focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 17

The Charenton papers lay squared to the edge of Vautrin's desk as neatly as cartridges. Blue office stock, red pencil, the asylum seal pressed into two of them so hard the paper had gone white around the stamp. Elise saw the total before she sat down. Three hundred and twelve francs overdue. Twenty-eight due again at the month's turn. Since the cart took Lucien east and the masons set to sealing the quarry mouth, her side pulled when she bent, the half-healed damage from the tollhouse catching under the stays of a dress that still smelled faintly of old smoke no matter how often she beat it out.

Vautrin remained standing for a moment, one hand on the back of his chair, giving the numbers room to begin the work before he spoke. His cuffs were white. The room beyond the glass partition was not. Clerks moved through heat and dust with ink on their thumbs and sweat dark under their arms, but his office held the stale cool of shut shutters and authority. Even the spittoon had been polished.

"Sit down, Mlle Valette," he said. "Standing will not change the sum."

She sat because the pain had already decided for her. "You stopped paying."

"I stopped advancing funds against an operative who had burned state material, vanished from supervision, and declined to deliver the map or the statement subsequently requested of her. The Charenton office was patient for a fortnight. It has now become exact."

He pushed the top sheet toward her with one clean finger. There was another paper beneath it, not a bill but a transfer note. If the arrears were not covered, her mother would be moved out of the paying wing and into the common women's ward. Elise could see the rest without the clerk's hand. She had once followed an attendant through the wrong door while looking for a...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...he story had already started moving without her.

"You heard him," Clemence said.

"Yes."

"If you bring them here before night, I won't wait for Lucien next time."

"I know."

Clemence studied her a second longer. Up close the lye had split the skin beside her thumbnails. There was flour on her sleeve from slicing bread and a new gray thread caught in the hair she had pinned up too fast. Little domestic accidents of a woman still keeping a world going while others broke it.

"Lucie asks after you," Clemence said. "I told her you were sleeping by the ration room because the inner chamber cough was keeping people awake. That lie is for her, not for you. Don't build anything on it."

Then she went back toward the schoolroom.

Elise sat on the pallet carefully, teeth set against the pull in her side. The alcove held ration-room air: cold bread, onion skin, lamp oil, the blunt soap Clemence shaved into the wash water, and blanket wool that never dried all the way in summer closeness. Voices carried straight through the narrow opening, close enough for her to hear bowls set down and too far to catch the words once they dropped low.

After a minute Lucien's voice reached her anyway, practical now, already moving beyond the wound she had made. He asked for the map. He asked who among the adults could still carry weight in a flood passage. He asked which children had shoes fit for water and which would need rags bound round their feet. Her name never entered it again.

Much later, after the bowls were washed and Lucien's low questions about flood passages and children's shoes had gone thin in the gallery, Elise eased off her boots and lay down fully dressed with one blanket folded under her cheek. The quarry never went fully still, but exhaustion took her before the voices did.
